title: Determine Whether AGV Or RMS Is Responsible For Observed Robot Behavior
summary: Use the source-described division of responsibilities between onboard AGV hardware and Geek+ RMS to classify whether an observed robot behavior is primarily associated with AGV sensing functions or RMS supervisory control, task queue, and routing functions.
when_to_use: Use when reviewing or discussing observed AGV behavior and you need a source-backed way to determine whether the behavior aligns with onboard AGV sensing responsibilities or RMS control and routing responsibilities.
not_for: Do not use this runbook to infer deeper robot logic or undocumented subsystem ownership beyond what the source states.
Do not use this runbook as a detailed troubleshooting or repair procedure, because the source provides only a high-level architecture explanation.
Do not use this runbook when the observed behavior does not fit the source-described AGV or RMS responsibilities without escalation.

roles_and_responsibilities.responsibility: Review the observed behavior and classify it against the source-described AGV versus RMS responsibility boundaries.

safety_notes: This source is a high-level training explanation and does not authorize physical intervention or control changes.
Do not infer undocumented controls, subsystem ownership, or recovery actions from this reference alone.
access_or_tools_needed: Description of the observed AGV behavior or condition
Source-backed AGV versus RMS responsibility mapping
Optional access to RMS for context

system_preconditions: An observed robot behavior or condition is available for review.

visual_references.description: Training slide/frame showing AGV versus RMS responsibilities, including RMS direct control, route determination, and AGV collision sensing.

steps.title: Identify the observed behavior
steps.purpose: Establish the specific robot behavior or condition that needs to be classified.
steps.instruction: Identify the observed robot behavior or condition being discussed, such as obstacle detection, movement execution, or route selection.
steps.expected_result: A specific behavior or condition is defined for comparison against the source-described responsibility split.
steps.healthy_condition: The behavior can be clearly described in terms of what the robot sensed, did, or was commanded to do.
steps.failure_condition: The behavior is too vague to compare to AGV sensing functions or RMS control functions.

actions.label: Define observed behavior
actions.exact_action: Identify the observed robot behavior or condition being discussed, such as obstacle detection, movement execution, or route selection.

stop_or_escalate_if: Escalate if the observed behavior cannot be clearly described well enough to compare against the source-described AGV and RMS responsibilities.

source_evidence.text: Identify the observed robot behavior or condition being discussed, such as obstacle detection, movement execution, or route selection.

source_evidence.quote_or_summary: Transcript explains that inside the AGV are sensors and collision sensing, which report back to RMS, while RMS dictates what the robot needs to do and sends commands.

steps.title: Compare the behavior to AGV sensing functions
steps.purpose: Determine whether the observed behavior aligns with onboard AGV hardware responsibilities described in the source.
steps.instruction: Compare the behavior to the source-described AGV functions: physical sensors, distance calculation, and collision sensing on the AGV.
steps.expected_result: You can determine whether the behavior matches onboard AGV sensing or detection functions.
steps.healthy_condition: The behavior aligns with AGV physical sensing, distance awareness, or collision sensing described in the source.
steps.failure_condition: The behavior does not align with AGV sensing functions or cannot be confidently mapped to them from the source.

actions.label: Check AGV responsibility match
actions.exact_action: Compare the behavior to the source-described AGV functions: physical sensors, distance calculation, and collision sensing on the AGV.

screens_or_images.what_to_look_at: Look for the explanation that AGVs have onboard physical sensors and forward collision sensors.

stop_or_escalate_if: Escalate if the behavior appears sensor-related but the source does not clearly support assigning it to AGV hardware.

source_evidence.text: Compare the behavior to the source-described AGV functions: physical sensors, distance calculation, and collision sensing on the AGV.

source_evidence.quote_or_summary: Transcript explains that inside the AGV are sensors and collision sensing, which report back to RMS, while RMS dictates what the robot needs to do and sends commands.
Slide text indicates the AGVs have forward collision sensors.

steps.title: Compare the behavior to RMS control functions
steps.purpose: Determine whether the observed behavior aligns with RMS supervisory control responsibilities described in the source.
steps.instruction: Compare the behavior to the source-described RMS functions: dictating what the robot needs to do, holding the task queue, sending commands to the AGV, and deciding routing from one station or point to another.
steps.expected_result: You can determine whether the behavior matches RMS tasking, command, or routing functions.
steps.healthy_condition: The behavior aligns with RMS task queue, command issuance, or route determination described in the source.
steps.failure_condition: The behavior does not align with RMS control functions or cannot be confidently mapped to them from the source.

actions.label: Check RMS responsibility match
actions.exact_action: Compare the behavior to the source-described RMS functions: dictating what the robot needs to do, holding the task queue, sending commands to the AGV, and deciding routing from one station or point to another.

screens_or_images.what_to_look_at: Look for the explanation that RMS dictates what the robot needs to do, maintains the queue, sends commands, and determines the best route from point A to point B.

stop_or_escalate_if: Escalate if the behavior appears related to tasking or routing but the source does not clearly support assigning it to RMS.

source_evidence.text: Compare the behavior to the source-described RMS functions: dictating what the robot needs to do, holding the task queue, sending commands to the AGV, and deciding routing from one station or point to another.

source_evidence.quote_or_summary: Transcript explains that inside the AGV are sensors and collision sensing, which report back to RMS, while RMS dictates what the robot needs to do and sends commands.
Slide and transcript state that Geek+ AGVs operate over QR codes, are directly controlled by Geek+ RMS, and Geek+ handles all movement and route determination.
The WCS assigns robot movement tasks. Those tasks are queued in RMS, and RMS executes them sequentially by sending commands to the AGV.

steps.title: Attribute obstacle or collision detection to AGV hardware
steps.purpose: Apply the source-described ownership when the observed condition involves obstacle sensing or collision detection.
steps.instruction: If the condition involves an obstacle seen by the robot or collision sensing, attribute the originating detection to AGV hardware and note that it reports back to RMS.
steps.expected_result: Obstacle or collision-related detection is classified as originating on the AGV, with reporting back to RMS.
steps.healthy_condition: The observed condition is clearly an onboard sensing or collision-detection function and is attributed to AGV hardware per the source.
steps.failure_condition: The condition involves obstacle or collision behavior but the source does not support a clear AGV-origin classification beyond high-level sensing responsibility.

actions.label: Assign obstacle detection ownership
actions.exact_action: If the condition involves an obstacle seen by the robot or collision sensing, attribute the originating detection to AGV hardware and note that it reports back to RMS.

screens_or_images.what_to_look_at: Look for the AGV collision sensor reference and the explanation that AGV sensing reports back to RMS.

stop_or_escalate_if: Escalate if the obstacle or collision-related condition appears to involve logic or ownership beyond onboard sensing and reporting.
Stop short of assigning deeper subsystem ownership not stated in the source.

source_evidence.text: If the condition involves an obstacle seen by the robot or collision sensing, attribute the originating detection to AGV hardware and note that it reports back to RMS.

source_evidence.quote_or_summary: Transcript explains that inside the AGV are sensors and collision sensing, which report back to RMS, while RMS dictates what the robot needs to do and sends commands.

steps.title: Attribute route, queue, or commanded movement to RMS
steps.purpose: Apply the source-described ownership when the observed condition involves routing, queued work, or commanded movement.
steps.instruction: If the condition involves route choice, queued tasks, or commanded movement from one location to another, attribute that responsibility to RMS.
steps.expected_result: Route, queue, or command-related behavior is classified as RMS responsibility.
steps.healthy_condition: The observed condition is clearly related to route determination, task queue management, or commands sent to the AGV and is attributed to RMS per the source.
steps.failure_condition: The condition appears related to routing or commands but the source does not support a clear RMS classification beyond the high-level architecture description.

actions.label: Assign RMS control ownership
actions.exact_action: If the condition involves route choice, queued tasks, or commanded movement from one location to another, attribute that responsibility to RMS.

screens_or_images.what_to_look_at: Look for the slide/frame showing Geek+ RMS directly controls AGVs and determines the best route from point A to point B.

stop_or_escalate_if: Escalate if the observed behavior does not fit the source-described AGV or RMS responsibilities.
Do not infer deeper robot logic or undocumented subsystem ownership beyond what the source states.

source_evidence.text: If the condition involves route choice, queued tasks, or commanded movement from one location to another, attribute that responsibility to RMS. [Image support needed: slide or frame showing Geek+ RMS directly controls AGVs and determines the best route from point A to point B.]

source_evidence.quote_or_summary: Slide and transcript state that Geek+ AGVs operate over QR codes, are directly controlled by Geek+ RMS, and Geek+ handles all movement and route determination.
The WCS assigns robot movement tasks. Those tasks are queued in RMS, and RMS executes them sequentially by sending commands to the AGV.
Artifact covers RMS responsibilities including direct AGV control, task queue, sending commands, and determining the best route from point A to point B.

success_criteria: The user can distinguish whether the observed behavior is associated with AGV onboard sensing functions or RMS control and routing functions.
Observed obstacle or collision sensing is attributed to AGV hardware reporting back to RMS when supported by the source.
Observed route choice, queued tasks, or commanded movement is attributed to RMS when supported by the source.
healthy_conditions: The observed behavior maps clearly to the source-described AGV sensing responsibilities or RMS control responsibilities.
The classification stays within the high-level architecture boundaries stated in the source.
failure_conditions: The observed behavior does not fit the source-described AGV or RMS responsibilities.
The reviewer attempts to infer undocumented subsystem ownership or deeper robot logic not supported by the source.
The source does not provide enough detail to classify the behavior confidently.
escalation_guidance: Escalate if the observed behavior does not fit the source-described AGV or RMS responsibilities, because the segment provides only a high-level architecture explanation.
Escalate if classification would require deeper robot logic or undocumented subsystem ownership beyond what the source states.

screens_or_images.what_to_look_at: Use this frame as the primary visual reference for AGV versus RMS responsibility boundaries, including AGV collision sensing and RMS route determination.

source_refs.quote_or_summary: Transcript explains that inside the AGV are sensors and collision sensing, which report back to RMS, while RMS dictates what the robot needs to do and sends commands.
Slide and transcript state that Geek+ AGVs operate over QR codes, are directly controlled by Geek+ RMS, and Geek+ handles all movement and route determination.

missing_details: The source provides a high-level architecture explanation only and does not define detailed troubleshooting decision trees.
The source does not provide commands, system paths, thresholds for classification, or formal escalation contacts.
The source does not define role boundaries beyond general support interpretation use.
